paper.title: Effect of aggregate type on properties of hardened self-consolidating lightweight concrete (SCLC)
paper.material_system: Self-consolidating concrete and self-consolidating lightweight concrete with limestone, pumice, tuff, or diatomite aggregate
paper.notes: The packet contains compressive-strength figures and text ranges but no complete mix-proportion table or exact numeric strength table.

strength_results.notes: SCC compressive strengths are plotted in Fig. 5 for five water/binder ratios and four ages. Exact water/binder-specific and age-specific values require figure digitization.
Pumice SCLC compressive strengths are plotted in Fig. 6. Exact water/binder-specific and age-specific values require figure digitization.
Tuff SCLC compressive strengths are plotted in Fig. 7. Exact water/binder-specific and age-specific values require figure digitization.
Diatomite SCLC compressive strengths are plotted in Fig. 8. Exact water/binder-specific and age-specific values require figure digitization.

conditions.applies_to: SCC and SCLC cube specimens before strength testing
conditions.curing_method: standard water curing until testing ages
conditions.notes: Packet states specimens were tested at ages but does not include full curing protocol.

conditions.applies_to: compressive strength of SCC and SCLC
conditions.test_method: compressive strength tested on 150 mm cube specimens by 2000 kN compressive machine with loading-rate controller
conditions.specimen_geometry: 150 mm x 150 mm x 150 mm cube

figures.caption: Fig. 5. Compressive strength of SCC versus curing time.
Fig. 6. Compressive strength of SCLC with pumice versus curing time.
Fig. 7. Compressive strength of SCLC with tuff versus curing time.
Fig. 8. Compressive strength of SCLC with diatomite versus curing time.

evidence.evidence_text: Effect of aggregate type on properties of hardened self-consolidating lightweight concrete (SCLC). Three coarse lightweight aggregate types, pumice, volcanic tuff and diatomite, and normal limestone aggregate were used.
The compressive and splitting tensile strength of SCC and SCLCs were tested on cube specimens (150 x 150 x 150 mm) aged 3, 7, 28 and 90 days according to TS EN 12390-3 and TS EN 12390-6.
evidence.supports: compressive-strength test ages, standard, and specimen geometry

evidence.evidence_text: Fig. 5 gives compressive strength of SCC versus curing time. Text reports SCC strength ranges of 27-35 MPa at 3 days, 38-47 MPa at 7 days, 41-52 MPa at 28 days and 45-51 MPa at 90 days depending on water-to-binder ratio.
evidence.supports: figure-only SCC compressive strength and text ranges

evidence.evidence_text: Figs. 6-8 present compressive strength of SCLC with pumice, tuff and diatomite versus curing time. Text reports, at w/b 0.36 depending on age, pumice 15-27 MPa, tuff 16-25 MPa and diatomite 10-22 MPa.
evidence.supports: figure-only SCLC compressive strength and text ranges

review_queue.question: Digitize Figs. 5-8 to obtain compressive strengths for each aggregate type, water/binder ratio, and age.
review_queue.suggested_action: Record exact or digitized plotted values and split the grouped review records into one row per mixture-age value.

main_risks: No exact numeric compressive-strength table is present in the packet.
Mix proportions are missing from the supplied packet, so mixture_components are not extracted.
Current strength rows are grouped figure-digitization placeholders and must be split after digitization.
